medical_exemption_details.contraindication_explanation:
Cadet Ethan A. McAllister has a documented history of severe immediate hypersensitivity reactions consistent with anaphylaxis to polyethylene glycol (PEG)–containing medications, which constitutes a contraindication to currently available mRNA COVID-19 vaccines under CDC/ACIP guidance.

History and supporting details:
1. In January 2019, the patient experienced an anaphylactic reaction within approximately 10 minutes of receiving an intramuscular injection of a depot methylprednisolone preparation that contained polyethylene glycol as an excipient. Symptoms included generalized urticaria, facial and periorbital swelling, throat tightness, wheezing, and hypotension (systolic blood pressure documented at 78 mmHg by EMS on arrival). He was treated emergently in the emergency department at Carilion Roanoke Memorial Hospital with intramuscular epinephrine, IV fluids, corticosteroids, and antihistamines, and was observed overnight. He was discharged with a diagnosis of anaphylaxis, suspected PEG excipient allergy, and was referred to Allergy/Immunology.

2. Subsequent allergy workup (February–April 2019) by Dr. Karen L. Whitfield, MD (Board-Certified Allergist/Immunologist) at Blue Ridge Allergy & Asthma Clinic, Roanoke, VA, included a detailed medication history and skin testing to PEG and polysorbate-containing agents. Skin-prick and intradermal testing demonstrated a positive response to PEG 3350 at non-irritant concentrations, with appropriate controls. Testing to polysorbate-80 was borderline/weakly positive at higher concentrations, and the working clinical diagnosis has been PEG allergy with possible cross-reactivity to polysorbates.

3. The patient has also reported episodes of generalized hives and lip swelling after oral intake of a PEG 3350–based laxative (MiraLAX) in late 2018, which resolved with oral antihistamines but did not require emergency care. This history further supports a clinically significant PEG hypersensitivity.

4. Based on this workup, the patient has been advised since 2019 to avoid medications and vaccines containing polyethylene glycol and, when possible, polysorbate excipients. He carries an epinephrine auto-injector at all times and has a written anaphylaxis action plan on file.

Relevance to COVID-19 vaccination:

• Both Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna mRNA COVID-19 vaccines contain polyethylene glycol (PEG) in their lipid nanoparticle formulations. Current CDC/ACIP recommendations and manufacturer prescribing information list a known severe allergic reaction (e.g., anaphylaxis) to any component of the vaccine, including PEG, as a contraindication to receiving that vaccine.

• Given the patient’s documented anaphylaxis to a PEG-containing injectable medication and positive allergy testing to PEG, administration of an mRNA COVID-19 vaccine would place him at high risk for another life-threatening anaphylactic event.

• While the adenoviral vector vaccines (e.g., Johnson & Johnson/Janssen) do not contain PEG, they do contain polysorbate-80, and there is recognized potential cross-reactivity between PEG and polysorbate in some highly sensitized individuals. Due to his borderline testing to polysorbate-80 and the severity of his prior reaction to PEG, our Allergy/Immunology service has recommended that any COVID-19 vaccination, including non-mRNA platforms currently available in the U.S., be deferred unless and until newer formulations without PEG or polysorbate are available, and then only under controlled conditions in a hospital setting with intensive monitoring and premedication.

• We have discussed the risks and benefits of COVID-19 vaccination with the patient and his family on multiple occasions. At this time, the risk of a severe immediate hypersensitivity reaction to currently available formulations is considered unacceptably high compared with the potential benefit, especially given that effective non-pharmacologic preventive measures (masking, distancing when appropriate, testing, and early treatment if infected) can be implemented in his case.

Summary:

Due to his well-documented, specialist-confirmed severe allergy to polyethylene glycol with possible polysorbate cross-reactivity, Mr. McAllister meets the medical criteria for a contraindication to currently available COVID-19 vaccines. Until alternative formulations without PEG or polysorbate are available and can be safely trialed in a controlled setting, I do not recommend COVID-19 vaccination for this cadet. This exemption should be considered valid for the foreseeable future, with reassessment if and when new vaccine formulations or desensitization protocols become available.

Please note that this exemption pertains specifically to COVID-19 vaccines that contain PEG and/or polysorbate. It does not apply to other immunizations unless they share these excipients and have been specifically reviewed by Allergy/Immunology.
